name: ANTLR4 Python Runtime (Parser Generator)
pricing_notes: Open source (BSD-3-Clause); Terence Parr / ANTLR project

auth.notes: Local library; grammar tool requires Java (antlr4 jar); runtime is pure Python

performance.notes: Python runtime slower than Java/C++ targets; use C++ runtime for production parsers

agent_readiness.notes: Full-featured parser generator for Python. Grammar: grammar Calc; expr: expr '+' term | term; term: INT. Generate: antlr4 -Dlanguage=Python3 Calc.g4. import antlr4. input_stream = antlr4.InputStream(text). lexer = CalcLexer(input_stream). stream = antlr4.CommonTokenStream(lexer). parser = CalcParser(stream). tree = parser.expr(). Visitor: class CalcVisitor(CalcVisitorBase): visitExpr(). Listener: class CalcListener(CalcListenerBase): enterExpr(). antlr4.ParseTreeWalker.DEFAULT.walk(listener, tree). Grammar reuse from 100+ public ANTLR4 grammars (C, Java, Python, SQL, etc.).

gotchas: Grammar tool requires Java — only runtime is Python
Python runtime is 10-100x slower than Java or C++ targets
Generated parser files must be regenerated when grammar changes
Complex grammar ambiguities require predicate-based disambiguation
Steep learning curve for grammar writing
best_when: Parsing existing language grammars (SQL, Java, C, Python) from 100+ available grammars
Building full compilers or transpilers with visitor/listener pattern
When lexer/parser separation and typed token streams are needed
Production language tooling (use C++ or Java target for performance)
avoid_when: Simple DSLs (use textX or Lark for pure Python, no Java required)
Quick parsing tasks (use pyparsing or parsimonious)
summary: ANTLR4 Python runtime runs parsers generated from .g4 grammars. Grammar tool requires Java; runtime is Python. antlr4.InputStream → Lexer → CommonTokenStream → Parser → ParseTree. Visitor/Listener pattern for tree walking. 100+ existing grammars (SQL, C, Java, Python). BSD-3 by Terence Parr. Best for full language parsing. Python runtime is 10-100x slower than Java/C++. Grammar changes require regeneration. For simple DSLs use Lark or textX.
